channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.description:

  4. China's Tianwen-2 probe close enough to start tests on asteroid 1 billion km away, CCTV reports  Yahoo
  5. Tianwen 2 rendezvouses with target asteroid, set to begin surveys  China Daily

  4. Why NASA Is Launching a Mission to Save a Quarter-Billion Dollar Space Telescope  Time Magazine
  5. NASA launches spacecraft to recover sinking telescope  DW.com

item.title: We Didn’t Scratch It! Hands-On With The Stone-Washed Montblanc Iced Sea Automatic Date 0 Oxygen - Fratello Watches
item.description: We Didn’t Scratch It! Hands-On With The Stone-Washed Montblanc Iced Sea Automatic Date 0 Oxygen  Fratello Watches

item.title: The Voyager 1 probe, launched in 1977, is now so far from Earth that a radio signal travelling at the speed of light takes over 22 hours to reach it, and it is still sending data back from interstellar space on a power source the size of a car battery - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The Voyager 1 probe, launched in 1977, is now so far from Earth that a radio signal travelling at the speed of light takes over 22 hours to reach it, and it is still sending data back from interstellar space on a power source the size of a car battery  Space Daily
  2. NASA Has Not Launched Anything Faster Than Voyager in 48 Years. Here Is the Real Menu of Machines That Could Finally Beat It  19FortyFive
  3. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 on a four-year mission, and in November 2026, nearly half a century later, it will become the first object ever to reach one light-day from Earth — so distant that a signal now takes a full 24 hours to cross the emptiness back home  19FortyFive
  4. In 2024, engineers rescued Voyager 1 after it spent months transmitting gibberish, remotely rewriting and relocating pieces of 46-year-old code around a failed memory chip from more than 24 billion kilometres away.  Space Daily
  5. Voyager 1 isn't dying because anything broke — after almost 50 years it's simply running out of power, and NASA is shutting off its instruments one by one until the signal goes silent.  19FortyFive

item.title: A nearby black hole as a window into the early universe - Phys.org
item.description:
1. A nearby black hole as a window into the early universe  Phys.org
  2. Rare, long-lasting black hole outburst is giving scientists a better look at the early universe  starlust.org

item.title: Mars once had a magnetic field strong enough to deflect solar wind, just as Earth's does now — when it collapsed, the planet lost its atmosphere and its oceans within a geologically brief period, transforming from a world that may have held life into the frozen d - Space Daily
item.description: Mars once had a magnetic field strong enough to deflect solar wind, just as Earth's does now — when it collapsed, the planet lost its atmosphere and its oceans within a geologically brief period, transforming from a world that may have held life into the frozen d  Space Daily

item.title: A horse can destroy a human in a sprint, but over a brutal long haul in summer heat, Daniel Lieberman argues the advantage can flip: humans keep cooling themselves through sweat, while a horse’s larger body struggles to shed heat fast enough. - Space Daily
item.description: A horse can destroy a human in a sprint, but over a brutal long haul in summer heat, Daniel Lieberman argues the advantage can flip: humans keep cooling themselves through sweat, while a horse’s larger body struggles to shed heat fast enough.  Space Daily

item.description:

  4. Scientists say Elon Musk’s orbital data centers could blind Earth’s biggest telescopes  Fast Company

item.title: A martian rock has lots of carbon on it, and it’s not clear why - Ars Technica
item.description:
1. A martian rock has lots of carbon on it, and it’s not clear why  Ars Technica
  2. Perseverance Finds Complex Organic Matter on Mars  Sci.News
  3. NASA’s Curiosity rover has identified the most diverse set of organic molecules yet found on Mars, including seven never before detected there, from a sample drilled in 2020 and studied for years before the chemistry finally came into focus  Space Daily
  4. Ancient Martian River Channel Yields Complex Organics  Universe Today
  5. Guest column: Did Earth life reach Mars?  pottsmerc.com

item.title: Human flight was still 7 years away in 1776. Now, we're headed back to the moon - Space
item.description:
1. Human flight was still 7 years away in 1776. Now, we're headed back to the moon  Space
  2. NASA’s Artemis II Breaks Agency Streaming Record  NASA (.gov)
  3. Moonbound memories  Wahpeton Daily News
  4. The record Apollo 13 set for the farthest humans had ever travelled from Earth was never meant to be a record — it was a survival manoeuvre after an explosion, and Artemis II quietly surpassed it on a clear April morning in 2026  Space Daily
  5. Take Me to the Moon: A Virtual Evening with NASA  Maine Public

item.title: James Webb has found evidence for thick water-ice clouds on Epsilon Indi Ab, one of the closest Jupiter-like worlds we can study directly — a discovery that didn’t overturn every atmospheric model, but exposed how many of them had been leaving out the ver - Space Daily
item.description: James Webb has found evidence for thick water-ice clouds on Epsilon Indi Ab, one of the closest Jupiter-like worlds we can study directly — a discovery that didn’t overturn every atmospheric model, but exposed how many of them had been leaving out the ver  Space Daily

item.title: In March 2025, Australia's new SKA-Low telescope photographed 85 galaxies using just 1,024 of its planned 131,072 antennas — less than one percent of its full power, and the image was already beyond what scientists hoped for - Space Daily
item.description: In March 2025, Australia's new SKA-Low telescope photographed 85 galaxies using just 1,024 of its planned 131,072 antennas — less than one percent of its full power, and the image was already beyond what scientists hoped for  Space Daily

item.title: For much of the past century, scientists believed dinosaurs were already in decline when the asteroid struck 66 million years ago — but a 2025 study published in Science found fossils in New Mexico showing they were flourishing in diverse, thriving ecosyste - Space Daily
item.description:
1. For much of the past century, scientists believed dinosaurs were already in decline when the asteroid struck 66 million years ago — but a 2025 study published in Science found fossils in New Mexico showing they were flourishing in diverse, thriving ecosyste  Space Daily
  2. How did cockroaches survive the asteroid that led to the extinction of dinosaurs?  KPVI
  3. Meteorite that killed dinosaurs also created life-supporting environment – study  Cotswold Journal

item.title: The James Webb Space Telescope has detected the same unexplained absorption signal on the surfaces of Pluto and Titan — two frozen worlds with very different environments but methane-and-nitrogen chemistry in common — and so far, scientists cannot - Space Daily
item.description: The James Webb Space Telescope has detected the same unexplained absorption signal on the surfaces of Pluto and Titan — two frozen worlds with very different environments but methane-and-nitrogen chemistry in common — and so far, scientists cannot  Space DailySee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: A University of Washington researcher published a finding in 2025 that stops you cold: blue whales sing at frequencies so deep that killer whales, their only natural predator, cannot hear them at all — their calls are invisible to orca ears beyond one kilometre - Space Daily
item.description: A University of Washington researcher published a finding in 2025 that stops you cold: blue whales sing at frequencies so deep that killer whales, their only natural predator, cannot hear them at all — their calls are invisible to orca ears beyond one kilometre  Space Daily

item.title: A genetic analysis of nearly 700,000 people identified 74 distinct locations in the human genome tied to anxiety, 39 of which had never been linked to the condition before - Space Daily
item.description: A genetic analysis of nearly 700,000 people identified 74 distinct locations in the human genome tied to anxiety, 39 of which had never been linked to the condition before  Space Daily

item.title: The debate over how many Earth-like planets exist has a wide range — from one, if the Rare Earth hypothesis is right, to something approaching a hundred quintillion, if Milky Way rates hold across the universe's roughly two trillion galaxies — and both answer - Space Daily
item.description: The debate over how many Earth-like planets exist has a wide range — from one, if the Rare Earth hypothesis is right, to something approaching a hundred quintillion, if Milky Way rates hold across the universe's roughly two trillion galaxies — and both answer  Space Daily

item.title: Saturn used to be listed with 83 moons. Then astronomers looked harder, found dozens more, and by 2026 the count had exploded to 285 — so many that Saturn now has nearly three times as many confirmed moons as Jupiter. - Space Daily
item.description: Saturn used to be listed with 83 moons. Then astronomers looked harder, found dozens more, and by 2026 the count had exploded to 285 — so many that Saturn now has nearly three times as many confirmed moons as Jupiter.  Space Daily

item.title: Astronomers may have caught an early galaxy in the process of dying - Phys.org
item.description: Astronomers may have caught an early galaxy in the process of dying  Phys.org

item.title: Two enormous structures — the Giant Arc and the Big Ring — appear in the same region of deep space at the same cosmic distance, a pairing so unexpected that Alexia Lopez, one of the astronomers who discovered them, says current cosmology struggles to - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Two enormous structures — the Giant Arc and the Big Ring — appear in the same region of deep space at the same cosmic distance, a pairing so unexpected that Alexia Lopez, one of the astronomers who discovered them, says current cosmology struggles to  Space Daily
  2. Mammoth Structure Found in Deep Space Challenges Our Understanding of The Universe  ScienceAlert
  3. A Massive Ring-Like Structure of Galaxies Defies the Universe's Rules — Here's Why It's Such a Mystery  Discover Magazine
  4. Giant structure in deep space calls fundamental laws of the Universe into question  NEWS.am TECH
  5. ATLANTIC SKIES: Why the discovery of two 'cosmic superstructures' is challenging a long-held scientific belief  PNI Atlantic News
